objects.abstract:
The OAuth 2.1 authorization framework enables an application to
   obtain limited access to a protected resource, either on behalf of a
   resource owner by orchestrating an approval interaction between the
   resource owner and an authorization service, or by allowing the
   application to obtain access on its own behalf.  This specification
   replaces and obsoletes the OAuth 2.0 Authorization Framework
   described in RFC 6749 and the Bearer Token Usage in RFC 6750.
objects.name: draft-ietf-oauth-v2-1
objects.title: The OAuth 2.1 Authorization Framework

objects.abstract:
Transaction Tokens (Txn-Tokens) are designed to maintain and
   propagate user identity, workload identity and authorization context
   throughout the Call Chain within a trusted domain during the
   processing of external requests (e.g. such as API calls) or requests
   initiated internally within the trust domain.  Txn-Tokens ensure that
   this context is preserved throughout the Call Chain thereby enhancing
   security and consistency in complex, multi-service architectures.
objects.name: draft-ietf-oauth-transaction-tokens
objects.title: Transaction Tokens

objects.abstract:
This specification defines a status mechanism called Token Status
   List (TSL), data structures and processing rules for representing the
   status of tokens secured by JSON Object Signing and Encryption (JOSE)
   or CBOR Object Signing and Encryption (COSE), such as JWT, SD-JWT,
   CBOR Web Token, and ISO mdoc.  It also defines an extension point and
   a registry for future status mechanisms.
objects.name: draft-ietf-oauth-status-list
objects.title: Token Status List (TSL)

objects.abstract:
This specification defines a mechanism to preserve identity and
   authorization information across trust domains that use the OAuth 2.0
   Framework.

Discussion Venues

   This note is to be removed before publishing as an RFC.

   Discussion of this document takes place on the Web Authorization
   Protocol Working Group mailing list (oauth@ietf.org), which is
   archived at https://mailarchive.ietf.org/arch/browse/oauth/.

   Source for this draft and an issue tracker can be found at
   https://github.com/oauth-wg/oauth-identity-chaining.
objects.name: draft-ietf-oauth-identity-chaining
objects.title: OAuth Identity and Authorization Chaining Across Domains

objects.abstract:
JSON Web Tokens, also known as JWTs, are URL-safe JSON-based security
   tokens that contain a set of claims that can be signed and/or
   encrypted.  JWTs are being widely used and deployed as a simple
   security token format in numerous protocols and applications, both in
   the area of digital identity and in other application areas.  This
   Best Current Practices (BCP) specification updates RFC 7519 to
   provide actionable guidance leading to secure implementation and
   deployment of JWTs.

   This BCP specification furthermore replaces the existing JWT BCP
   specification RFC 8725 to provide additional actionable guidance
   covering threats and attacks that have been discovered since RFC 8725
   was published.
objects.name: draft-ietf-oauth-rfc8725bis
objects.title: JSON Web Token Best Current Practices

objects.abstract:
This document describes threats against cross-device flows along with
   practical mitigations, protocol selection guidance, and a summary of
   formal analysis results identified as relevant to the security of
   cross-device flows.  It serves as a security guide to system
   designers, architects, product managers, security specialists, fraud
   analysts and engineers implementing cross-device flows.
objects.name: draft-ietf-oauth-cross-device-security
objects.title: Cross-Device Flows: Security Best Current Practice

objects.abstract:
This specification provides a mechanism for an application to use an
   identity assertion to obtain an access token for a third-party API by
   coordinating through an identity provider that the downstream
   Resource Authorization Server already trusts for single sign-on
   (SSO), using Token Exchange [RFC8693] and JWT Profile for OAuth 2.0
   Authorization Grants [RFC7523].
objects.name: draft-ietf-oauth-identity-assertion-authz-grant
objects.title: Identity Assertion JWT Authorization Grant

objects.abstract:
This specification defines an extension to the OAuth 2.0 protocol
   [RFC6749] that enables a client instance to include a key-bound
   attestation when interacting with an Authorization Server or Resource
   Server.  This mechanism allows a client instance to prove its
   authenticity verified by a client attester without revealing its
   target audience to that attester.  It may also serve as a mechanism
   for client authentication as per OAuth 2.0.
objects.name: draft-ietf-oauth-attestation-based-client-auth
objects.title: OAuth 2.0 Attestation-Based Client Authentication

objects.abstract:
This specification describes data formats as well as validation and
   processing rules to express Verifiable Digital Credentials with JSON
   payloads with and without selective disclosure based on the SD-JWT
   format.
objects.name: draft-ietf-oauth-sd-jwt-vc
objects.title: SD-JWT-based Verifiable Digital Credentials (SD-JWT VC)

objects.abstract:
This specification defines a mechanism through which an OAuth client
   can identify itself to authorization servers, without prior dynamic
   client registration or other existing registration.  This is through
   the usage of a URL as a client_id in an OAuth flow, where the URL
   refers to a document containing the necessary client metadata,
   enabling the authorization server to fetch the metadata about the
   client as needed.
objects.name: draft-ietf-oauth-client-id-metadata-document
objects.title: OAuth Client ID Metadata Document

objects.abstract:
This document defines the Authorization Challenge Endpoint, which
   supports clients that want to control the process of obtaining
   authorization from the user using a native experience.

   In many cases, this can provide an entirely browserless OAuth 2.0
   experience suited for native applications, only delegating to the
   browser in unexpected, high risk, or error conditions.
objects.name: draft-ietf-oauth-first-party-apps
objects.title: OAuth 2.0 for First-Party Applications

objects.abstract:
This specification profiles the Assertion Framework for OAuth 2.0
   Client Authentication and Authorization Grants [RFC7521], the JWT
   Profile for OAuth 2.0 Client Authentication and Authorization Grants
   [RFC7523], and OAuth 2.0 Attestation-Based Client Authentication
   [I-D.draft-ietf-oauth-attestation-based-client-auth] to enable the
   use of SPIFFE Verifiable Identity Documents (SVIDs) as client
   credentials in OAuth 2.0.  It defines how OAuth clients with SPIFFE
   credentials can authenticate to OAuth authorization servers using
   their JWT-SVIDs, WIT-SVIDs, or X.509-SVIDs without the need for
   client secrets.  This approach enhances security by enabling seamless
   integration between SPIFFE-enabled workloads and OAuth authorization
   servers while eliminating the need to distribute and manage shared
   secrets such as static client secrets.
objects.name: draft-ietf-oauth-spiffe-client-auth
objects.title: OAuth SPIFFE Client Authentication

objects.abstract:
This document updates the set of best current security practices for
   OAuth 2.0 by extending the security advice given in RFC 6749, RFC
   6750, and RFC 9700, to cover new threats that have been discovered
   since the former documents have been published.
objects.name: draft-ietf-oauth-security-topics-update
objects.title: Updates to OAuth 2.0 Security Best Current Practice

objects.abstract:
This specification details the threats, attack consequences, security
   considerations and best practices that must be taken into account
   when developing browser-based applications that use OAuth 2.0.

Discussion Venues

   This note is to be removed before publishing as an RFC.

   Discussion of this document takes place on the Web Authorization
   Protocol Working Group mailing list (oauth@ietf.org), which is
   archived at https://mailarchive.ietf.org/arch/browse/oauth/.

   Source for this draft and an issue tracker can be found at
   https://github.com/oauth-wg/oauth-browser-based-apps.
objects.name: draft-ietf-oauth-browser-based-apps
objects.title: OAuth 2.0 for Browser-Based Applications

objects.abstract:
This specification extends OAuth 2.0 [RFC6749] by adding new token
   endpoint response parameters to specify refresh token expiration and
   user authorization expiration.
objects.name: draft-ietf-oauth-refresh-token-expiration
objects.title: OAuth 2.0 Refresh Token and Authorization Expiration

objects.abstract:
This document updates RFC7521, RFC7522, RFC7523 and RFC9126 with
   respect to the treatment of audience values in OAuth 2.0 Client
   Assertion Authentication and Assertion-based Authorization Grants to
   address a security vulnerability identified in the previous
   requirements for those audience values in multiple OAuth 2.0
   specifications.
objects.name: draft-ietf-oauth-rfc7523bis
objects.title: Updates to OAuth 2.0 JSON Web Token (JWT) Client Authentication and Assertion-Based Authorization Grants

objects.comments: 1st meeting at 73rd IETF-Minneapolis, MN; 2nd meeting at 74th IETF-San Francisco, CA
objects.description:
The Web Authorization (OAuth) protocol allows a user to grant a
third-party web site or application access to the user's protected
resources, without necessarily revealing their long-term credentials,
or even their identity. For example, a photo-sharing site that
supports OAuth could allow its users to use a third-party printing web
site to print their private pictures, without allowing the printing
site to gain full control of the user's account and without having the
user share his or her photo-sharing sites' long-term credential with
the printing site.
objects.name: Web Authorization Protocol

objects.desc: The BOF request is proposed
objects.name: Proposed

objects.desc: The BOF request is approved
objects.name: Approved

objects.desc: The BOF request is declined
objects.name: Declined

objects.desc: The BOF request is replaced
objects.name: Replaced

objects.desc: The BOF request is abandoned
objects.name: Abandoned

objects.desc: The BOF request is spam
objects.name: Spam

objects.desc: This charter's group was replaced.
objects.name: Replaced

objects.desc: The proposed charter is not being considered at this time. A proposed charter will remain in this state until an AD moves it to Start Chartering/Rechartering (Internal IESG/IAB Review). This state is useful for drafting the charter, discussing with chairs, etc.
objects.name: Draft Charter

objects.desc: This is the state when you'd like to propose the charter / new charter. This state also allows you to ask whether external review can be skipped in ballot. After you select this state, the Secretariat takes over and drives the rest of the process.
objects.name: Start Chartering/Rechartering (Internal Steering Group/IAB Review)

objects.desc: This state is selected by the Secretariat (AD's, keep yer grubby mits off this!) when it has been decided that the charter needs external review.
objects.name: External Review (Message to Community, Selected by Secretariat)

objects.desc: This state is selected by the Secretariat (AD's, keep yer grubby mits off this!) when the IESG is reviewing the discussion from the external review of the proposed charter (this is similar to the IESG Evaluation state for a draft).
objects.name: IESG Review (Charter for Approval, Selected by Secretariat)

objects.desc: The charter is approved by the IESG.
objects.name: Approved

objects.desc: The proposed charter is not being considered at this time. A proposed charter will remain in this state until an AD moves it to Informal IESG review.
objects.name: Not currently under review

objects.desc: A conflict review has been requested, but a shepherding AD has not yet been assigned
objects.name: Needs Shepherd

objects.desc: The sponsoring AD is reviewing the document and preparing a proposed response
objects.name: AD Review

objects.desc: The IESG is considering the proposed conflict review response
objects.name: IESG Evaluation

objects.desc: The evaluation of the proposed conflict review response has been deferred to the next telechat
objects.name: IESG Evaluation - Defer

objects.desc: The IESG has approved the conflict review response (a request to not publish), but a point has been raised that should be cleared before moving to announcement to be sent
objects.name: Approved Request to Not Publish - point raised

objects.desc: The IESG has approved the conflict review response, but a point has been raised that should be cleared before proceeding to announcement to be sent
objects.name: Approved No Problem - point raised

objects.desc: The IESG has approved the conflict review response (a request to not publish), but the secretariat has not yet sent the response
objects.name: Approved Request to Not Publish - announcement to be sent

objects.desc: The IESG has approved the conflict review response, but the secretariat has not yet sent the response
objects.name: Approved No Problem - announcement to be sent

objects.desc: The secretariat has delivered the IESG's approved conflict review response (a request to not publish) to the requester
objects.name: Approved Request to Not Publish - announcement sent

objects.desc: The secretariat has delivered the IESG's approved conflict review response to the requester
objects.name: Approved No Problem - announcement sent

objects.desc: The request for conflict review was withdrawn
objects.name: Withdrawn

objects.desc: The conflict review has been abandoned
objects.name: Dead

objects.desc: A new document has been received by IANA, but no actions have been taken
objects.name: New Document

objects.desc: IANA is currently processing the actions for this document
objects.name: In Progress

objects.desc: IANA is waiting on the document's authors to respond
objects.name: Waiting on Authors

objects.desc: IANA is waiting on the IETF Area Directors to respond
objects.name: Waiting on ADs

objects.desc: IANA is waiting on the IETF Working Group Chairs to respond
objects.name: Waiting on WGC

objects.desc: IANA has notified the RFC Editor that the actions have been completed
objects.name: Waiting on RFC Editor

objects.desc: Request completed. The RFC Editor has acknowledged receipt of IANA's message that the actions have been completed
objects.name: RFC-Ed-Ack

objects.desc: IANA has suspended work on the document
objects.name: On Hold

objects.desc: Request completed. There were no IANA actions for this document
objects.name: No IANA Actions

objects.desc: One or more registries need experts assigned
objects.name: Need IANA Expert(s)

objects.desc: One or more expert reviews have been assigned
objects.name: Reviews assigned

objects.desc: All expert reviews have been completed with no blocking issues
objects.name: Expert Reviews OK

objects.desc: Some expert reviewers have identified issues
objects.name: Issues identified

objects.desc: Document has not yet been reviewed by IANA.
objects.name: IANA - Review Needed

objects.desc: Document requires IANA actions, and the IANA Considerations section indicates the details of the actions correctly.
objects.name: IANA OK - Actions Needed

objects.desc: Document requires no IANA action, and the IANA Considerations section indicates this correctly.
objects.name: IANA OK - No Actions Needed

objects.desc: IANA has issues with the text of the IANA Considerations section of the document and/or needs to request expert approval for registry actions.
objects.name: IANA - Not OK

objects.desc:
Document has changed after review by IANA. This state is set automatically.

IANA is not notified when documents are updated.

IANA reviews documents during IETF Last Call, during IESG Evaluation, and before the IESG telechat. If a review is required after the telechat (or before Last Call), please contact IANA at iana@iana.org.
objects.name: Version Changed - Review Needed

objects.desc: The IESG has not started processing this draft, or has stopped processing it without publication.
objects.name: I-D Exists

objects.desc: A formal request has been made to advance/publish the document, following the procedures in Section 7.5 of RFC 2418. The request could be from a WG chair, from an individual through the RFC Editor, etc. (The Secretariat (iesg-secretary@ietf.org) is copied on these requests to ensure that the request makes it into the ID tracker.) A document in this state has not (yet) been reviewed by an AD nor has any official action been taken on it yet (other than to note that its publication has been requested.
objects.name: Publication Requested

objects.desc: A specific AD (e.g., the Area Advisor for the WG) has begun reviewing the document to verify that it is ready for advancement. The shepherding AD is responsible for doing any necessary review before starting an IETF Last Call or sending the document directly to the IESG as a whole.
objects.name: AD Evaluation

objects.desc: An AD sometimes asks for an external review by an outside party as part of evaluating whether a document is ready for advancement. MIBs, for example, are reviewed by the "MIB doctors". Other types of reviews may also be requested (e.g., security, operations impact, etc.). Documents stay in this state until the review is complete and possibly until the issues raised in the review are addressed. See the "note" field for specific details on the nature of the review.
objects.name: Expert Review

objects.desc: The AD has requested that the Secretariat start an IETF Last Call, but the the actual Last Call message has not been sent yet.
objects.name: Last Call Requested

objects.desc: The document is currently waiting for IETF Last Call to complete. Last Calls for WG documents typically last 2 weeks, those for individual submissions last 4 weeks.
objects.name: In Last Call

objects.desc: Before a standards-track or BCP document is formally considered by the entire IESG, the AD must write up a protocol action. The protocol action is included in the approval message that the Secretariat sends out when the document is approved for publication as an RFC.
objects.name: Waiting for Writeup

objects.desc: As a result of the IETF Last Call, comments may need to be responded to and a revision of the ID may be needed as well. The AD is responsible for verifying that all Last Call comments have been adequately addressed and that the (possibly revised) document is in the ID directory and ready for consideration by the IESG as a whole.
objects.name: Waiting for AD Go-Ahead

objects.desc: The document is now (finally!) being formally reviewed by the entire IESG. Documents are discussed in email or during a bi-weekly IESG telechat. In this phase, each AD reviews the document and airs any issues they may have. Unresolvable issues are documented as "discuss" comments that can be forwarded to the authors/WG. See the description of substates for additional details about the current state of the IESG discussion.
objects.name: IESG Evaluation

objects.desc: During a telechat, one or more ADs requested an additional 2 weeks to review the document. A defer is designed to be an exception mechanism, and can only be invoked once, the first time the document comes up for discussion during a telechat.
objects.name: IESG Evaluation - Defer

objects.desc: The IESG has approved the document for publication, but the Secretariat has not yet sent out on official approval message.
objects.name: Approved-announcement to be sent

objects.desc: The IESG has approved the document for publication, and the Secretariat has sent out the official approval message to the RFC editor.
objects.name: Approved-announcement sent

objects.desc: The document is in the RFC editor Queue (as confirmed by https://www.rfc-editor.org/queue.html).
objects.name: RFC Ed Queue

objects.desc: The ID has been published as an RFC.
objects.name: RFC Published

objects.desc: Do Not Publish: The IESG recommends against publishing the document, but the writeup explaining its reasoning has not yet been produced. DNPs apply primarily to individual submissions received through the RFC editor.  See the "note" field for more details on who has the action item.
objects.name: DNP-waiting for AD note

objects.desc: The IESG recommends against publishing the document, the writeup explaining its reasoning has been produced, but the Secretariat has not yet sent out the official "do not publish" recommendation message.
objects.name: DNP-announcement to be sent

objects.desc: An AD is aware of the document and has chosen to place the document in a separate state in order to keep a closer eye on it (for whatever reason). Documents in this state are still not being actively tracked in the sense that no formal request has been made to publish or advance the document. The sole difference between this state and "I-D Exists" is that an AD has chosen to put it in a separate state, to make it easier to keep track of (for the AD's own reasons).
objects.name: AD is watching

objects.desc: Document is "dead" and is no longer being tracked. (E.g., it has been replaced by another document with a different name, it has been withdrawn, etc.)
objects.name: Dead

objects.desc: Approved by the stream manager (e.g., IESG, IAB, IRSG, ISE), awaiting processing and publishing
objects.name: EDIT

objects.desc: Awaiting IESG action
objects.name: IESG

objects.desc: Document has been edited, but is holding for completion of IANA actions
objects.name: IANA

objects.desc: Approved by the stream manager (e.g., IESG, IAB, IRSG, ISE), awaiting processing and publishing
objects.name: EDIT

objects.desc: Awaiting final author approval
objects.name: AUTH48

objects.desc: Awaiting author action
objects.name: AUTH

objects.desc: Final approvals are complete
objects.name: AUTH48-DONE

objects.desc: Final approvals are complete
objects.name: AUTH48-DONE

objects.desc: RFC-Editor/IANA Registration Coordination
objects.name: IANA

objects.desc: Holding for IESG action
objects.name: IESG

objects.desc: Independent Submission awaiting author update, or in discussion between author and ISE
objects.name: ISR-AUTH

objects.desc: Tooling Issue; an update is needed to one or more of the tools in the publication pipeline before this document can be published
objects.name: TI

objects.desc: Awaiting missing normative reference
objects.name: MISSREF

objects.desc: Time-out period during which the IESG reviews document for conflict/concurrence with other IETF working group work
objects.name: TO

objects.desc: Awaiting final RFC Editor review before AUTH48
objects.name: RFC-EDITOR

objects.desc: Holding for normative reference
objects.name: REF

objects.desc: Independent submission awaiting author action, or in discussion between author and ISE
objects.name: ISR-AUTH

objects.desc: Independent Submission Review by the ISE
objects.name: ISR
Editorial stream document under RSAB review

objects.desc: The editorial stream processing of this document is complete and it has been sent to the RFC Editor for publication. The document may be in the RFC Editor's queue, or it may have been published as an RFC; this state doesn't distinguish between different states occurring after the document has left the RSAB.
objects.name: Sent to the RFC Editor

objects.desc: Replaced
objects.name: Replaced

objects.desc: A document being considered for the IAB stream.
objects.name: Candidate IAB Document

objects.desc: This document has been adopted by the IAB and is being actively developed.
objects.name: Active IAB Document

objects.desc: This document has lost its author or editor, is waiting for another document to be written, or cannot currently be worked on by the IAB for some other reason. Annotations probably explain why this document is parked.
objects.name: Parked IAB Document

objects.desc: This document has completed internal consensus within the IAB and is now under community review.
objects.name: Community Review

objects.desc: The document has completed community review and is now being formally reviewed by the entire IAB in order to make a decision about whether it is ready to be published as an IAB-stream RFC.
objects.name: IAB Review

objects.desc: The consideration of this document is complete, but it has not yet been sent to the RFC Editor for publication (although that is going to happen soon).
objects.name: Approved by IAB, To Be Sent to RFC Editor

objects.desc: The IAB does not expect to publish the document itself, but has passed it on to a different organization that might continue work on the document. The expectation is that the other organization will eventually publish the document.
objects.name: Sent to a Different Organization for Publication

objects.desc: The IAB processing of this document is complete and it has been sent to the RFC Editor for publication. The document may be in the RFC Editor's queue, or it may have been published as an RFC; this state doesn't distinguish between different states occurring after the document has left the IAB.
objects.name: Sent to the RFC Editor

objects.desc: The document has been published as an RFC.
objects.name: Published RFC

objects.desc: This document was an active IAB document, but for some reason it is no longer being pursued for the IAB stream. It is possible that the document might be revived later, possibly in another stream.
objects.name: Dead IAB Document

objects.desc: The individual submission document has been marked by the Working Group (WG) chairs as a candidate for adoption by the WG, but no adoption call has been started.
objects.name: Candidate for WG Adoption

objects.desc: A call for adoption of the individual submission document has been issued by the Working Group (WG) chairs.  This call is still running but the WG has not yet reached consensus for adoption.
objects.name: Call For Adoption By WG Issued

objects.desc: The individual submission document has been adopted by the Working Group (WG), but some administrative matter still needs to be completed (e.g., a WG document replacing this document with the typical naming convention of 'draft-ietf-wgname-topic-nn' has not yet been submitted).
objects.name: Adopted by a WG

objects.desc: The document is adopted by the Working Group (WG) for its internal use.  The WG has decided that it will not pursue publication of it as an RFC.
objects.name: Adopted for WG Info Only

objects.desc: The document has been identified as a Working Group (WG) document and is under development per Section 7.2 of RFC2418.
objects.name: WG Document

objects.desc: The Working Group (WG) document is in a temporary state where it will not be actively developed.  The reason for the pause is explained via a datatracker comments section.
objects.name: Parked WG Document

objects.desc: The Working Group (WG) document has been abandoned by the WG.  No further development is planned in this WG.  A decision to resume work on this document and move it out of this state is possible.
objects.name: Dead WG Document

objects.desc: The Working Group (WG) document is currently subject to an active WG Last Call (WGLC) review per Section 7.4 of RFC2418.
objects.name: In WG Last Call

objects.desc: The progression of this Working Group (WG) document towards publication is paused as it awaits implementation. The process governing the approach to implementations is WG-specific.
objects.name: Waiting for Implementation

objects.desc: Held by Working Group (WG) chairs for administrative reasons.  See document history for details.
objects.name: Held by WG

objects.desc: The Working Group (WG) document has completed Working Group Last Call (WGLC), but the WG chairs are not yet ready to call consensus on the document. The reasons for this may include comments from the WGLC need to be responded to, or a revision to the document is needed.
objects.name: Waiting for WG Chair Go-Ahead

objects.desc: The Working Group (WG) document has consensus to proceed to publication.  However, the document is waiting for a document shepherd write-up per RFC4858.
objects.name: WG Consensus: Waiting for Write-Up

objects.desc: The Working Group (WG) document has been submitted to the Internet Engineering Steering Group (IESG) for evaluation and publication per Section 7.4 of RFC2418.  See the “IESG State” or “RFC Editor State” for further details on the state of the document.
objects.name: Submitted to IESG for Publication

objects.desc: IRSG Review
objects.name: IRSG Review

objects.desc: Replaced
objects.name: Replaced

objects.desc: This document is under consideration in an RG for becoming an IRTF document. A document in this state does not imply any RG consensus and does not imply any precedence or selection.  It's simply a way to indicate that somebody has asked for a document to be considered for adoption by an RG.
objects.name: Candidate RG Document

objects.desc: This document has been adopted by the RG and is being actively developed.
objects.name: Active RG Document

objects.desc: This document has lost its author or editor, is waiting for another document to be written, or cannot currently be worked on by the RG for some other reason.
objects.name: Parked RG Document

objects.desc: The document is in its final review in the RG.
objects.name: In RG Last Call

objects.desc: IRTF documents have document shepherds who help RG documents through the process after the RG has finished with the document.
objects.name: Waiting for Document Shepherd

objects.desc: The IRTF Chair is meant to be performing some task such as sending a request for IESG Review.
objects.name: Waiting for IRTF Chair

objects.desc: The document shepherd has taken the document to the IRSG and solicited reviews from one or more IRSG members.
objects.name: Awaiting IRSG Reviews

objects.desc: The IRSG is taking a poll on whether or not the document is ready to be published.
objects.name: In IRSG Poll

objects.desc: The IRSG has asked the IESG to do a review of the document, as described in RFC5742.
objects.name: In IESG Review

objects.desc: The RG processing of this document is complete and it has been sent to the RFC Editor for publication. The document may be in the RFC Editor's queue, or it may have been published as an RFC; this state doesn't distinguish between different states occurring after the document has left the RG.
objects.name: Sent to the RFC Editor

objects.desc: The document has been published as an RFC.
objects.name: Published RFC

objects.desc: The IESG has requested that the document be held pending further review, as specified in RFC 5742, and the IRTF has agreed to such a hold.
objects.name: Document on Hold Based On IESG Request

objects.desc: This document was an active IRTF document, but for some reason it is no longer being pursued for the IRTF stream. It is possible that the document might be revived later, possibly in another stream.
objects.name: Dead IRTF Document

objects.desc: Replaced
objects.name: Replaced

objects.desc: The draft has been sent to the ISE with a request for publication.
objects.name: Submission Received

objects.desc: The ISE is finding initial reviewers for the document.
objects.name: Finding Reviewers

objects.desc: The ISE is actively working on the document.
objects.name: In ISE Review

objects.desc: One or more reviews have been sent to the author, and the ISE is awaiting response.
objects.name: Response to Review Needed

objects.desc: The ISE has asked the IESG to do a review of the document, as described in RFC5742.
objects.name: In IESG Review

objects.desc: The ISE processing of this document is complete and it has been sent to the RFC Editor for publication. The document may be in the RFC Editor's queue, or it may have been published as an RFC; this state doesn't distinguish between different states occurring after the document has left the ISE.
objects.name: Sent to the RFC Editor

objects.desc: The document has been published as an RFC.
objects.name: Published RFC

objects.desc: This document was actively considered in the Independent Submission stream, but the ISE chose not to publish it.  It is possible that the document might be revived later. A document in this state may have a comment explaining the reasoning of the ISE (such as if the document was going to move to a different stream).
objects.name: No Longer In Independent Submission Stream

objects.desc: The IESG has requested that the document be held pending further review, as specified in RFC 5742, and the ISE has agreed to such a hold.
objects.name: Document on Hold Based On IESG Request

objects.desc: The material is active
objects.name: Active

objects.desc: The material is removed
objects.name: Removed

objects.desc: This document is not active, but is available in the archives
objects.name: Archived

objects.desc: An RFC status change has been requested, but a shepherding AD has not yet been assigned
objects.name: Needs Shepherd

objects.desc: The sponsoring AD is preparing an RFC status change document
objects.name: AD Review

objects.desc: Last Call has been requested for this proposed status change
objects.name: Last Call Requested

objects.desc: This proposed status change is in IETF Last Call
objects.name: In Last Call

objects.desc: The AD is following up on IETF LC comments
objects.name: Waiting for AD Go-Ahead

objects.desc: The IESG is considering the proposed RFC status changes
objects.name: IESG Evaluation

objects.desc: The evaluation of the proposed RFC status changes have been deferred to the next telechat
objects.name: IESG Evaluation - Defer

objects.desc: The IESG has approved the RFC status changes, but a point has been raised that should be cleared before proceeding to announcement to be sent
objects.name: Approved - point raised

objects.desc: The IESG has approved the RFC status changes, but the secretariat has not yet sent the announcement
objects.name: Approved - announcement to be sent

objects.desc: The secretariat has announced the IESG's approved RFC status changes
objects.name: Approved - announcement sent

objects.desc: The RFC status changes have been abandoned
objects.name: Dead

objects.desc: The statement is active
objects.name: Active

objects.desc: The statement has been marked historic
objects.name: Historic

objects.desc: The statement is no longer active
objects.name: Inactive

objects.desc: The statement has been replaced
objects.name: Replaced
